children.name: Microcarbo
children.description: Pygmy cormorants — small, long-tailed cormorants of freshwater lakes and rivers in Africa, southern Asia, and Australasia. The smallest members of the cormorant family, they have proportionally longer tails and shorter bills than their larger relatives. Pygmy cormorants are highly gregarious, often nesting in mixed colonies with herons and ibises, and they employ a cooperative feeding strategy where groups drive fish into shallows before diving.

children.name: Microcarbo pygmaeus
children.description: The smallest European cormorant, barely larger than a moorhen, with a long cocked tail and a small rounded head. In breeding plumage the head and neck turn a rich chestnut-brown with fine white speckling, contrasting with the glossy black body. Breeds colonially in dense reedbeds across the Danube delta, Balkan lakes, and east to the Caspian region, with the population expanding northward in recent decades.

children.name: Microcarbo africanus
children.description: A small, slender cormorant with a long thin bill and a diagnostic white throat extending down the foreneck in breeding plumage. Widespread across sub-Saharan Africa on fresh waters from open lakes to narrow forest streams, it is the most common cormorant in much of the continent. Unlike many cormorants, it readily perches on telegraph wires and can often be seen roosting alongside African darters.

children.name: Microcarbo coronatus
children.description: A small, dark cormorant endemic to the cold Benguela Current coastline of southwestern Africa, from central Namibia to the Cape of Good Hope. It has a short black crest on the crown and a red-orange facial patch that intensifies during breeding. The species is restricted to rocky inshore waters and nests in small colonies on offshore islands, often alongside bank and Cape cormorants.

children.name: Microcarbo niger
children.description: A diminutive cormorant of the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia, easily identified by its small size, short bill, and white throat in breeding dress. It frequents freshwater wetlands, flooded fields, and village ponds, often associating with little egrets and openbill storks. Little cormorants are adept at catching small fish in shallow water, using rapid surface dives that last barely ten seconds.

children.name: Microcarbo melanoleucos
children.description: A small pied cormorant found across Australasia, from Indonesia and New Guinea to New Zealand and throughout Australia. Its stark black-and-white plumage — white underparts with black upperparts and a short black crest — is distinctive among the small cormorants. It frequents both coastal waters and inland wetlands, and in New Zealand it is known as the little shag and is one of the country's most familiar waterbirds.

children.name: Poikilocarbo
children.description: Red-legged cormorant — a monotypic genus found along the Pacific coast of South America from northern Peru to Tierra del Fuego. Easily distinguished from all other cormorants by its vivid coral-red legs and feet, which contrast sharply with its grey-blue plumage and orange throat patch. The red-legged cormorant is a specialist of exposed rocky shores and strong tidal currents, where it forages in the surf zone among breaking waves.

children.name: Poikilocarbo gaimardi
children.description: The only cormorant with vivid coral-red legs and feet, a feature that gives it the local Spanish name 'pato rojo' — red duck. Its plumage is a soft grey-blue with a white throat and neck patch, and a bright orange-yellow gape. Unlike most cormorants, it builds a nest almost entirely of seaweed glued to vertical cliff faces with its own guano, often on exposed headlands battered by Pacific surf. It is classified as Near Threatened due to guano harvesting disturbance and El Niño-driven prey crashes.

children.name: Urile
children.description: North Pacific cormorants — a genus of four species that breed on the cold coasts and islands of the northern Pacific Rim, from Japan and Kamchatka across the Aleutians to western North America. They have distinctive bare facial skin patterns and, in most species, a bright blue or purple gape. One species, the spectacled cormorant, is the only cormorant to have gone extinct in historical times, vanishing from the Commander Islands around 1850.

children.name: Urile penicillatus
children.description: A large, heavy-bodied cormorant of the Pacific coast of North America from British Columbia to Baja California. In breeding plumage it develops a distinct buff throat patch and fine white plumes on the neck, while the bare gape turns a brilliant blue. It nests on rocky offshore islands in dense colonies that can number tens of thousands, and its population is highly sensitive to oil spills and El Niño-related food shortages.

children.name: Urile urile
children.description: Striking for its bright red-orange bare facial skin extending from the bill to the eye, framed by a pale blue-white ring. It breeds from the Commander and Aleutian Islands east to the Alaska Peninsula, typically on rocky cliffs in small, loose colonies. The red-faced cormorant is one of the least studied cormorants because of its remote subarctic nesting sites and the difficulty of accessing its cliff-edge colonies.

children.name: Urile pelagicus
children.description: The smallest of the North Pacific cormorants, with a slender neck, long tail, and a distinctive thin bill. In breeding plumage it develops a purple-green sheen and white flank patches that flash during display, along with bright red facial skin. It nests on the narrowest cliff ledges, often in solitary pairs rather than colonies, and forages in nearshore waters with remarkable agility, chasing fish through kelp forests and rocky reefs.

children.name: Urile perspicillatus
children.description: The only cormorant species known to have gone extinct in historical times. It was a large, flightless or weak-flying cormorant endemic to Bering Island in the Commander Islands, with distinctive glassy-lensed white patches around the eyes that gave it the 'spectacled' name. Hunted to extinction by sailors and introduced predators by the 1850s, it was first described by Georg Steller in 1741 and is known today from only a handful of museum specimens and his detailed accounts.

children.name: Phalacrocorax
children.description: Great cormorants and shags — large, fish-eating waterbirds whose distinctive habit of spreading their wings to dry after diving is one of the most familiar sights at coasts and inland waters worldwide. Cormorants lack the fully waterproofed plumage of ducks and grebes, instead using partially water-permeable feathers that reduce buoyancy and help them pursue fish at depth — but require post-dive drying. This genus includes the most widespread and familiar cormorant species found across every continent except South America, ranging from the world's largest cormorant colony to species restricted to single island groups.

children.name: Phalacrocorax neglectus
children.description: A robust, dark-plumaged cormorant endemic to the Benguela upwelling zone of Namibia and western South Africa. It lacks the white belly or throat of other southern African cormorants and has a distinctive red-orange gape that becomes vivid during breeding. Bank cormorants build conspicuous nests of seaweed and guano on offshore rocks and islands, and the species is Endangered due to fishing pressure on its prey and disturbance at breeding colonies.

children.name: Phalacrocorax nigrogularis
children.description: A large, all-black cormorant with a slight green gloss and a distinctive yellow-orange throat patch, breeding only on islands in the Persian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. It forms some of the largest cormorant colonies in the world, with a single breeding site on the Hawar Islands of Bahrain hosting up to 200,000 pairs. The species is classified as Vulnerable due to oil spills, coastal development, and disturbance of its restricted breeding islands.

children.name: Phalacrocorax featherstoni
children.description: A medium-sized, grey-and-white shag endemic to the Chatham Islands of New Zealand, with a long slender bill and a double crest during breeding. It is restricted to rocky coastlines on Pitt Island and several smaller islets, where it feeds in shallow inshore waters. With a population of fewer than 1,000 individuals, it is classified as Endangered and threatened by introduced predators and fisheries bycatch.

children.name: Phalacrocorax punctatus
children.description: A slender, elegant shag endemic to New Zealand, named for the distinctive black-and-white dappled pattern on its back and wings during breeding. It has a long, thin grey bill and a double crest — one swept forward, one backward — that gives it a striking appearance at coastal roosts. Spotted shags nest in large colonies on vertical sea cliffs and are known for synchronised group flights in long, undulating ribbons along the coast.

children.name: Phalacrocorax fuscescens
children.description: A medium-sized cormorant endemic to southern and southwestern Australia, with a diagnostic black facial mask extending from bill to eye that contrasts with its white underparts. It is strictly marine, inhabiting rocky coasts and offshore islands, and rarely ventures into fresh water. Unlike most cormorants, the black-faced cormorant nests in loose colonies on the ground rather than on cliffs, building shallow seaweed-lined scrapes on island tops.

children.name: Phalacrocorax varius
children.description: A large pied cormorant with white underparts, a black back, and a prominent white throat patch that extends to the eye. It ranges widely across coastal and inland waters of Australia and New Zealand, where it is known as the pied shag. The species nests in colonies in trees or on the ground, and has been recorded using the same nesting sites for over a century in some New Zealand coastal forests.

children.name: Phalacrocorax sulcirostris
children.description: A small, slender all-black cormorant with a slim hooked bill and a distinctive greenish gloss to the plumage. It is the most widespread Australian cormorant, found on fresh water across the continent and also in New Guinea, Timor, and New Zealand. Little black cormorants forage in large coordinated flocks that drive fish into shallow water, a behaviour known as 'cooperative feeding', and can form feeding aggregations numbering in the thousands.

children.name: Phalacrocorax fuscicollis
children.description: A medium-sized cormorant of the Indian subcontinent, with a slender neck, narrow bill, and a distinctive white throat patch that extends below the gape in breeding plumage. It is primarily a freshwater species, inhabiting rivers, lakes, and reservoirs, and is often seen in mixed flocks with little cormorants and darters. Indian cormorants nest colonially in trees, often alongside painted storks and herons in large multi-species heronries.

children.name: Phalacrocorax capensis
children.description: A glossy blackish-green cormorant with a striking orange-yellow throat patch, endemic to the Benguela Current along the coasts of Namibia and South Africa. It is the most numerous cormorant of the region, forming enormous breeding colonies that historically numbered over a million birds on guano islands. The species is classified as Endangered due to a catastrophic population decline of over 60% driven by overfishing of sardines and anchovies and the collapse of its prey base.

children.name: Phalacrocorax capillatus
children.description: A large, dark cormorant with a glossy blue-black sheen, white throat patches, and a short crest. It breeds on rocky coasts of Japan, Korea, Sakhalin, and the Kuril Islands, and is a skilled diver that pursues fish in inshore and offshore waters. Japanese cormorants have a long cultural history in Japan, where traditional 'ukai' (cormorant fishing) on the Nagara River uses captive birds of this species to catch ayu sweetfish — a practice over 1,300 years old.

children.name: Phalacrocorax carbo
children.description: The most widespread and familiar cormorant species, found across Eurasia, Africa, Australia, and eastern North America. A large, heavy-bodied bird with a hooked bill and a white throat patch, it is equally at home on coasts and inland lakes and rivers. Highly efficient fish predators — a single bird can eat 500–700 g of fish daily — making them the subject of intense fisheries conflict in many regions. The post-dive wing-spreading posture is their most recognisable behaviour.

children.name: Gulosus
children.description: European shag — a monotypic genus separated from Phalacrocorax by molecular studies, restricted to the temperate and boreal coastal waters of Europe and North Africa. The European shag is distinguished from the great cormorant by its smaller size, more slender build, iridescent green-black plumage, and a distinctive forward-curling crest in breeding plumage. Unlike the great cormorant, it is strictly a marine species that rarely if ever ventures onto fresh water.

children.name: Gulosus aristotelis
children.description: Smaller and more iridescent than the great cormorant, with a distinctive forward-curling crest in breeding season. Its emerald-green iridescent plumage and yellow gape distinguish it clearly from the larger cormorant. Strictly a saltwater species that breeds on rocky sea cliffs and forages in shallow, rocky coastal waters for sand eels, sprats, and gobies. Dives in shallower, rockier water than the great cormorant.

children.name: Nannopterum
children.description: American cormorants — a genus of three species restricted to the Americas, including the only flightless cormorant in the world. These cormorants have undergone rapid evolutionary diversification in the New World, from the Galápagos flightless cormorant to the widespread double-crested and neotropic cormorants. The genus was long lumped into Phalacrocorax but molecular studies confirm its distinctness, with the flightless cormorant representing an extreme adaptation to an island environment without terrestrial predators.

children.name: Nannopterum harrisi
children.description: The only cormorant that cannot fly, endemic to the Galápagos Islands of Fernandina and Isabela. Its wings are reduced to about one-third the size needed for flight, but it is a powerful swimmer and diver that pursues fish, eels, and octopus in the cold Cromwell Current. The species has one of the smallest geographic ranges of any bird, with an estimated population of only about 1,000 pairs, and its vestigial wings are a striking example of evolution on isolated oceanic islands.

children.name: Nannopterum brasilianum
children.description: The only cormorant found widely in the Neotropics, ranging from the southern United States through Central America and the Caribbean to Tierra del Fuego. It is smaller than the double-crested cormorant, with a longer tail and a distinctive yellow-orange gape and throat pouch. Neotropic cormorants are highly adaptable and have expanded their range northward significantly in recent decades, colonising the Mississippi River basin as far north as Oklahoma.

children.name: Nannopterum auritum
children.description: The most widespread cormorant of North America, found from the Aleutian Islands to the Atlantic coast and inland across the Great Lakes and Mississippi watershed. Named for the two small feather tufts on its head that appear during breeding, it has a bright orange-yellow throat patch and a greenish-black gloss to its plumage. The species' population rebounded dramatically after being devastated by DDT in the mid-20th century, and it has since become abundant enough to cause conflicts with fisheries and aquaculture across its range.

children.name: Leucocarbo
children.description: Blue-eyed shags — a southern-hemisphere genus of medium to large cormorants found around the Antarctic and subantarctic islands, the southern coasts of South America, and New Zealand. They are named for the vivid blue ring around the eye that most species display, a feature unique among cormorants. Many Leucocarbo species have extremely restricted ranges, confined to single island groups, making them among the most range-restricted of all seabirds and particularly vulnerable to introduced predators.

children.name: Leucocarbo magellanicus
children.description: A small blue-eyed shag of southern South America, ranging from central Chile and Argentina south to Tierra del Fuego. It has black upperparts, white underparts, a white wing patch, and the characteristic bright blue orbital ring of the genus. Rock shags nest in large colonies on rocky cliffs and offshore islands and feed in coastal waters, often in the turbulent waters of the Strait of Magellan.

children.name: Leucocarbo bougainvilliorum
children.description: A large, black-and-white cormorant of the Humboldt Current off Peru and northern Chile, with white underparts, black upperparts, and red facial skin at the base of the bill. It is the most important seabird producer of guano in the world — its dense colonies on the Chincha and Ballestas Islands accumulated deposits of nitrogen-rich guano up to 50 metres deep, driving a 19th-century mining boom that reshaped the Peruvian economy. The species is classified as Near Threatened due to guano harvesting disturbance and El Niño-related prey crashes.

children.name: Leucocarbo ranfurlyi
children.description: A blue-eyed shag endemic to the remote Bounty Islands, a tiny windswept archipelago 670 km east of New Zealand. It has black upperparts, white underparts, and a blue orbital ring, but is most notable for the prominent yellow-orange caruncles on the base of the bill. With a population numbering only several thousand pairs confined to fewer than 20 bare granite islets, it is considered Vulnerable and one of the most range-restricted of all cormorant species.

children.name: Leucocarbo carunculatus
children.description: A large, heavy-built shag endemic to New Zealand's Marlborough Sounds, with black-and-white plumage, blue eye rings, and prominent orange wattles at the base of the bill. It is one of the rarest cormorants in the world, with a population of around 800 breeding pairs restricted to just five rocky islets in the Cook Strait region. The species is extremely sensitive to disturbance and has become a flagship conservation species for marine reserve planning in the Marlborough Sounds.

children.name: Leucocarbo onslowi
children.description: A medium-sized shag endemic to the Chatham Islands of New Zealand, with a distinctive double crest and extensive white plumage on the underparts and neck. It nests in small colonies on coastal rocks and headlands, feeding in the productive waters around the Chatham Rise. The species is classified as Critically Endangered with a population of fewer than 1,000 individuals, threatened by introduced predators, fisheries bycatch, and the degradation of its coastal breeding habitat.

children.name: Leucocarbo chalconotus
children.description: A large, sexually dimorphic shag endemic to southern New Zealand, with males having black-and-white plumage and females being entirely dark bronze-green. It breeds on small islands around Stewart Island and the Otago coast, nesting in dense colonies on grassy slopes and rocky headlands. The species exhibits a remarkable colour polymorphism — some populations contain both pied and entirely bronze individuals, a trait shared with only a few other cormorant species.

children.name: Leucocarbo colensoi
children.description: A slender, crested shag endemic to the subantarctic Auckland Islands south of New Zealand, with the characteristic blue eye ring and a slender black crest. It nests in small colonies among tussock grass on coastal slopes and forages in the rich waters of the Auckland Island shelf. With a total population of around 10,000 pairs spread across several islands, it is considered Vulnerable due to its restricted range and the risk of introduced predators reaching its breeding islands.

children.name: Leucocarbo campbelli
children.description: A medium-sized shag confined to the remote subantarctic Campbell Island group south of New Zealand, with black upperparts, white underparts, and a distinct white wing patch. It nests on coastal cliffs and rocky outcrops, feeding largely on small fish and squid in the surrounding Southern Ocean waters. The species is listed as Vulnerable, with the entire population of around 8,000 pairs vulnerable to accidental introduction of rats or mice to its sole breeding islands.
children.namedAfter: Campbell Island (named after Captain John Campbell)

children.name: Leucocarbo atriceps
children.description: A large, striking black-and-white shag of southern South America, found from the Falkland Islands and Tierra del Fuego north along both coasts of Patagonia. It has a distinctive white cheek and throat, a white wing patch, and the bright blue orbital ring characteristic of the genus. Imperial shags build tall, cone-shaped nests of seaweed cemented by guano on coastal cliffs and islands, forming dense colonies that sometimes number tens of thousands.

children.name: Leucocarbo georgianus
children.description: A blue-eyed shag endemic to South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands in the subantarctic South Atlantic. It has the characteristic black-and-white plumage of the Leucocarbo genus, with a prominent white cheek patch and dark blue orbital skin. The species was historically persecuted for guano but has recovered to an estimated population of about 10,000 pairs, and it is one of the few bird species that breeds on the Antarctic convergence zone.

children.name: Leucocarbo melanogenis
children.description: A blue-eyed shag endemic to the Crozet Islands and Prince Edward Islands in the southern Indian Ocean. It has a distinctive dark face extending below the eye, from which it takes its specific name, and the characteristic blue orbital ring of the genus. Crozet shags nest in small colonies on coastal cliffs and feed on fish and marine invertebrates in the nutrient-rich waters surrounding these remote subantarctic islands.

children.name: Leucocarbo bransfieldensis
children.description: The most southerly breeding cormorant, nesting on the Antarctic Peninsula and adjacent islands as far south as 68°S. It has black upperparts with a blue-green gloss, white underparts, and its most remarkable adaptation: a salt-excreting gland between the eyes that allows it to drink seawater. Antarctic shags are one of only a few bird species that breed in Antarctica alongside penguins and skuas, and they nest on snow-free coastal cliffs in small, widely dispersed colonies.

children.name: Leucocarbo verrucosus
children.description: A blue-eyed shag endemic to the Kerguelen Islands in the southern Indian Ocean, named for the prominent warty orange caruncles on the base of its bill. It has a dark blue-black head contrasting with white underparts and the characteristic blue eye ring of the genus. The species nests on coastal cliffs and is a strong swimmer, feeding on fish and benthic invertebrates in the cold, fertile waters of the Kerguelen Plateau.

children.name: Leucocarbo nivalis
children.description: A white-chested shag endemic to Heard Island and the McDonald Islands in the southern Indian Ocean. Its plumage is similar to other blue-eyed shags but with an extensive white throat and breast, and it has pinkish feet that contrast with the dark subantarctic rocks. Heard Island is one of the most remote places on Earth, and this species is one of the least studied cormorants, with basic aspects of its breeding biology and population dynamics still poorly understood.

children.name: Leucocarbo purpurascens
children.description: A medium-sized shag endemic to subantarctic Macquarie Island, lying roughly halfway between New Zealand and Antarctica. It has a black head, white underparts, a white wing patch, and the distinctive blue eye ring of the Leucocarbo genus. Macquarie Island was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site and its shag population has rebounded following the successful eradication of rats, mice, and cats from the island in one of the largest and most ambitious island restoration projects ever undertaken.
